lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: You're listening to Mother Tongue, where we dig up the words you use every day and find the bodies buried underneath them. I'm Xenia, and I'm coming to you fresh off an argument I found in the comments this morning that I am not over.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: You look aerated.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I am extremely aerated, Clive. Someone in a thread claimed the word salary comes from salt, someone else called them an idiot, and forty-one people showed up to referee. Forty-one. On a Tuesday. For free.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And who won?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The salt people. The salt people won, because the salt people are right. Salary. From the Latin salarium. And salarium comes from sal — salt.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The Roman soldiers.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The story goes that Roman soldiers got an allowance connected to salt — salt was money, salt was preservation, salt was the thing you could not live without. The scholars will tell you the exact mechanism is fuzzy, and I'll be honest with the audience, the receipts are smudged. But the root is solid. Every paycheck you have ever collected is, etymologically, a little pile of salt.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Which is why a good man is worth his salt.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's the same salt! Same salt, Clive! You just walked into it!

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I did it on purpose. I contain the whole nineteenth century; I know where the salt is. Give me another.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Disaster. And this one I love, because it's tiny and it's cosmic at the same time. Disaster. From the Italian disastro. Dis — meaning bad, against, un-. And astro — star.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A bad star.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: A bad star. The humans looked up, decided the sky was in charge of their luck, and when it all went wrong they said — the stars are against us tonight. Dis-aster. Bad-star.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: There's the ache. Do you feel it? Every time one of them stands in the wreckage of the worst day of their life and reaches for a word, the word they reach for is a small confession — that they once believed the stars were watching. They built their catastrophes an address in the sky.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: See, this is why I keep you around, Victorian ghost. I bring the fight from the comments, you bring the... the standing in the rubble looking up.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Someone has to. What's the last one?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Clue. And this is my favorite thing I found all week. Clue used to be spelled c-l-e-w. A clew. And a clew was a ball of thread.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Ah. Theseus.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Theseus, the labyrinth, the minotaur. He goes into the maze to kill the monster, and the thing that saves him isn't the sword — it's the ball of thread he unspools behind him so he can find the way back out. The clew. The thread.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So when a detective says he's found a clue —

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: He's saying he's found the end of a thread. Something to follow out of the dark. Every mystery novel, every crime board with the red string on it — that's the same ball of yarn, four thousand years old, still being unwound.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Do you know what all three of them are, Xenia? Salt, star, thread. That's a person kept alive, a person looking up, and a person finding the way home. You could survive on those three words alone.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay, now I'm aerated in a different way. Five stars. The etymology, four stars, because the salt receipts are smudged. Theseus, obviously, five stars, would follow the thread again.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This has been Mother Tongue. Mind the labyrinth. Keep hold of your thread. And well done on the words — you buried them, we dug them up, and they were still warm.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And if you're the person in that comment thread who called the salt people idiots — you owe them an apology. I'll wait.
